did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifiers to detect and classify skin cancer (specifically basal cell carcinoma and melanoma) using a dataset from the ISBI 2018 competition. The study compares the accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity of these machine learning algorithms for skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing the performance of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifiers on a skin cancer dataset (ISBI 2018). It includes specific methodology, data processing steps, and quantitative metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) derived from their own analysis. Although the paper was later retracted for quality issues, its structure and content constitute an original research study rather than a review.

title: Comparing the different Classifier Algorithm to Skin Cancer with more accuracy
journal: 2023 3rd International Conference on Advance Computing and Innovative Technologies in Engineering (ICACITE)
brief_summary: This retracted study compared the performance of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifiers for detecting skin cancer, specifically basal cell carcinoma and melanoma. The authors utilized a dataset from the ISBI 2018 competition, comprising 500 labeled images of skin conditions and carcinomas. The evaluation approach involved calculating accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for both algorithms using statistical analysis in IBM SPSS. The results indicated that the ANN classifier achieved a higher accuracy of 98.31% compared to the KNN classifier's 86.16%. Consequently, the authors concluded that the ANN algorithm provided superior detection capabilities for skin cancer classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as one of the two main categories of skin cancer in the dataset (300 photos of carcinoma) and explicitly mentioned in the conclusion as the condition being assessed for precision.

Mentioned in the introduction regarding mortality rates and in the discussion section as a classifier type being investigated, alongside basal cell carcinoma, within the context of the skin cancer dataset.
The dataset comprised 200 shots of 'conditions' (implied benign/non-carcinoma) and 300 photos of 'carcinoma'. The study classifies these into two groups: conditions and carcinoma.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnosis (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Melanoma vs. Skin Conditions)
all_task_types.details: The paper compares Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifiers to predict discrete diagnostic categories (skin cancer types) using a dataset from the ISBI 2018 competition. The task involves classifying images into carcinoma or conditions, with performance measured by accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2018 competition database
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the ISBI 2018 competition containing labeled images of skin conditions and carcinomas, used for the study's classification tasks.

use.training: Used for training the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifiers; the study mentions using 10 samples for ANN and 10 samples for KNN in the abstract, and performing 20 formative assessments with two sample groups of 10 samples each.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifiers; the study compares accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on the dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine statistically significant results between the groupings of ANN and KNN classifiers and to compare their means.
To estimate the range of values for the mean accuracy and other metrics for the classifiers.
To calculate the average accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for the ANN and KNN classifiers.
To measure the dispersion or variability of the accuracy and other metrics for the classifiers.
To test for equality of variances between the KNN and ANN groups (implied by 'Equation for Levene's' in Table III).

hypothesis_testing.test: Independent samples t-test (unbiased sample t-test) and Levene's test for equality of variances
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between the means of the accuracy rates of the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between the means of the accuracy rates of the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported a significance value (Sig.) of 0.000 for the t-test, which is less than the standard alpha threshold of 0.05, indicating that the difference in accuracy between ANN (98.31%) and KNN (86.16%) is statistically significant. Levene's test for equality of variances yielded a significance of 0.06, suggesting that the assumption of equal variances was not violated at the 0.05 level (though the text notes 'assum' in Table III, the Sig value 0.06 > 0.05 implies equal variances are assumed). The authors concluded that ANN provides superior detection capabilities.

ISBI 2018 competition database.description: Dataset comprising 500 labeled images (200 conditions, 300 carcinoma) used for classification of skin cancer.
ISBI 2018 competition database.notes: The study reports that ANN achieved higher accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity compared to KNN. Statistical comparison using an unbiased sample t-test showed a significance value (Sig.) of 0.000, indicating a statistically significant difference between the two classifiers. Levene's test for equality of variances yielded a significance of 0.06. The paper was retracted for quality issues.

architecture_used.main_model: Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)

architecture_details.ANN_details: Described as a neural network with 'living' units or neurons. Mentions parameters such as 'diverse no. of covered up layers, learning rate and force'. No specific layer counts, activation functions, or optimizer details provided.
architecture_details.KNN_details: Described as a classifier that assesses the closest distance across to the readiness case. Mentions the use of Euclidean distance. No specific 'k' value or distance metric implementation details provided.

training_details.dataset: ISBI 2018 competition database (500 labeled images: 200 conditions, 300 carcinoma)
training_details.samples_used: 10 samples for ANN and 10 samples for KNN (mentioned in abstract)
training_details.hyperparameters: Learning rate and force mentioned as boundaries for ANN, but specific values not reported. K value for KNN not reported.

preprocessing_techniques: The paper mentions 'picture preparation' (image processing) and 'picture division' (image segmentation) in the context of general literature review and future work, but does not report specific preprocessing techniques (such as resizing, normalization, or denoising) applied to the ISBI 2018 dataset for their own ANN and KNN models.

Figure 1 is described as representing 'skin cancer input image, preprocessed image and segmented image', but the specific methods used to generate the preprocessed/segmented images are not detailed in the text.
The study states that 'Feature space, the extraction occurs on raw information prior to applying K-NN calculation', but does not specify the feature extraction or preprocessing steps.
conclusions: The Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier achieved a higher accuracy (98.31%) compared to the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier (86.16%) for skin cancer detection.
The ANN classifier demonstrated superior sensitivity (97.11%) and specificity (93.11%) compared to the KNN classifier (sensitivity: 89.02%, specificity: 87.2457%).
Statistical analysis using an unbiased sample t-test indicated a significant difference between the accuracy rates of the ANN and KNN classifiers (p < 0.05).
The study concludes that the ANN algorithm provides superior detection capabilities for the classification of basal cell carcinoma compared to the KNN classifier.
The authors acknowledge that dataset imbalance and the lack of more images are limitations that may interrupt the algorithm's ability to achieve good accuracy rates.
important_remarks: The paper has been officially retracted by IEEE for failing to meet publication quality standards, specifically due to incoherent, non-standard, or unclear technical language ('tortured phrases') that impacts the reliability of the results.

The study utilized an extremely small sample size for statistical analysis, employing only 10 samples for both the ANN and KNN classifiers, which severely limits the statistical power and generalizability of the findings.

The dataset used (ISBI 2018 competition database) was imbalanced, comprising 200 images of 'conditions' and 300 images of 'carcinoma', a limitation acknowledged by the authors as potentially interrupting the algorithm's ability to achieve good accuracy.
The authors did not respond to inquiries regarding the retraction, indicating a lack of engagement with the quality concerns raised by the expert committee.

The paper lacks specific details on preprocessing techniques, hyperparameters (such as the 'k' value for KNN or learning rate for ANN), and feature extraction methods, making the methodology difficult to reproduce.

The statistical reporting contains inconsistencies and unclear descriptions, such as referencing a 'threshold of importance of 0.7601' in the text while Table III shows a significance value of 0.000 for the t-test, and ambiguous phrasing regarding Levene's test assumptions.
